questionText: Disprove the statement: ``Any two rectangles with the same area have the same perimeter.''

steps.description: Read the claim as a universal statement.
steps.explanation: The claim asserts equal area forces equal perimeter; two equal-area rectangles with different perimeters disprove it.
We need a single pair of rectangles with the same area but different perimeters.
We choose area $12$ and find two different rectangles achieving it.
Both $1\times12$ and $3\times4$ have area $12$, but very different shapes.
The long thin rectangle has perimeter $26$.
The more compact rectangle has perimeter $14$.
Same area, but the perimeters differ substantially.
Area fixes the product of the sides, but perimeter depends on their sum, which grows as the rectangle is stretched.
The $1\times12$ and $3\times4$ rectangles have equal area $12$ but perimeters $26$ and $14$, so the claim is disproved.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: A $1 \times 12$ rectangle and a $3 \times 4$ rectangle both have area $12$, but their perimeters are $26$ and $14$. Hence the statement is false. $\blacksquare$
